summary: Gruber compares the economics of Apple's product line, noting that buying both a 12" iBook and a 17" iMac costs less than a single 17" PowerBook while covering more use cases. He argues that for users who spend most of their time at a desk, the two-machine combo offers better value than one do-everything laptop. He highlights the iBook line as an exceptional bargain, noting that a capable portable for $1,100 was remarkable given that PowerBooks recently cost $3,000+. He extends the value-comparison theme to Apple's display line, citing Nathaniel Irons' observation that two smaller Cinema Displays offer more screen area than one 23" for the same price.
key_points: A 12" iBook plus a 17" iMac cost $100 less than a single 17" PowerBook while providing both a desktop and a portable
The 17" PowerBook and iMac were remarkably comparable in display resolution and processor performance, with only minor differences in bus speed and ports
For users at their desk 90%+ of the time, the two-machine approach eliminates paying a premium for portability they rarely use
The iBook line represented exceptional value at $1,100 compared to $3,000+ PowerBooks just a few years earlier
The same value-math applies to Apple's display line, where two smaller panels beat one large one in both area and cost
money_quotes: You can buy a low-end iBook and a 17" iMac — complete with extra RAM and an Airport card for the iBook — for less than the cost of a 17" PowerBook.
It certainly seems very tempting, to me at least, to walk out with two new computers rather than a lone use-it-everywhere PowerBook.
The iBook doesn't have a SuperDrive, but the iMac does — do you burn DVDs on the road that frequently?
Once you're off the desk, the lunchtray PowerBook is indisputably a more powerful machine with a much nicer display.
The iBook is two pounds lighter and runs cooler — making it a better machine for literal lap-top use.
I mention this comparison not to damn the 17" PowerBook for being overpriced, because it isn't (or at least not by much). What I think is noteworthy is the value in the iBook line.
Can you tell I just bought an iBook?
key_insight: The best value in Apple's lineup often comes from combining two less expensive products rather than buying one premium device, because the price premium for top-tier specs rises faster than the actual utility gained.
